commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain normal scripts using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `defer` using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `async` using a new example and justify the result.
Choose and justify execution-order trade-offs using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Normal scripts** → **`defer`** → **`async`** → **Execution-order trade-offs**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: Normal scripts is best understood as follows: A classic script without async or defer blocks HTML parsing while it is fetched and executed.
choices.label: A classic script without async or defer blocks HTML parsing while it is fetched and executed.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `async`.
choices.label: Turn a problem into observable inputs, outputs, constraints, and smaller transformations. Describe behavior before syntax, then identify the smallest step that can be tested independently.

choices.feedback: `async` is best understood as follows: An async external script downloads in parallel and executes as soon as it is ready, without preserving order relative to other async scripts.
choices.label: An async external script downloads in parallel and executes as soon as it is ready, without preserving order relative to other async scripts.

questions.explanation: `async` is best understood as follows: An async external script downloads in parallel and executes as soon as it is ready, without preserving order relative to other async scripts.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.

choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
choices.label: Apply Normal scripts deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Normal scripts, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.
questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Efficient Script Loading?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Avoid Efficient Script Loading completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use Efficient Script Loading when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Efficient Script Loading.
choices.label: Use Efficient Script Loading in every file because more abstraction is always better.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Efficient Script Loading?

researchSources.title: Efficient Script Loading: defer and async
researchSources.usage: Coverage research only; no transcript wording is canonical.

sections.body: A classic script without async or defer blocks HTML parsing while it is fetched and executed.
sections.title: Normal scripts

sections.body: A deferred external script downloads in parallel, executes after parsing, and preserves document order among deferred scripts.
sections.title: `defer`

sections.body: An async external script downloads in parallel and executes as soon as it is ready, without preserving order relative to other async scripts.
sections.title: `async`

sections.body: Choose script loading based on parser blocking, DOM readiness, dependency order, and independence rather than download speed alone.
sections.title: Execution-order trade-offs

sections.explanation: The independent script can execute when ready; the application script waits for parsed HTML and preserves deferred order.
sections.title: Worked example

summary: Efficient Script Loading connects Normal scripts, `defer`, `async`.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: Efficient Script Loading

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in efficient script loading recur throughout later JavaScript work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **DOM Internals and Script Loading** module.
